1: Vayyishmaˁ Pashhur ben-Immer hakohen vehu fagid nagid beyt YHWH et Yirmeyahu mitnabbe et-hadevarim haele — Pashhur son of Immer, the priest who was chief officer in the house of YHWH, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things. Nāgîd (chief officer, leader) — the administrative head of the temple; this is not the same Pashhur as in ch21 (son of Malkijah vs. son of Immer). The institutional authority that will suppress Jeremiah is rooted in the temple hierarchy itself.
2: Vayach Pashhur et Yirmeyahu hanavi vayiten oto el-hamahpechet — Pashhur struck (hiḵkâ, Hiphil causative) Jeremiah the prophet and put him in the stocks (mahpeḵet, an instrument of physical confinement and pain). Mahpeḵet comes from hāpaḵ (to turn/twist) — it is a device that holds the body in an unnatural twisted position. The location, shaˁar Binyamin haˁelyon asher beveyt YHWH (the Upper Benjamin Gate of the temple), places the humiliation publicly within the temple precincts.
3: Vayehi mimmohorat vayotse Pashhur et Yirmeyahu min-hamahpechet — the next day Pashhur released Jeremiah from the stocks. Jeremiah's response is the prophetic renaming: lo Pashhur qara YHWH shemecha ki im-Magor Missaviv — YHWH has not named you Pashhur but Magor Missaviv, Terror-on-Every-Side. Māgôr missāḇîḇ (terror from all around) — the phrase that recurs in 6:25 and 20:10 as a terror-cry becomes the new identity of the man who thought to terrorize the prophet. Prophetic renaming in the OT constitutes a new reality, not merely a new label.
4: Ki koh amar YHWH hineni notench lemahgor lecha ulchol-ohhavecha — for thus says YHWH: I am making you a terror to yourself and to all your friends (ʾōhăḇêkā, those who love/support you). Venaplu beherev oyvehem veˁeinecha roˁot — they will fall by the sword of their enemies while your eyes watch. The specific horror of the prophecy: Pashhur will live to see his friends slaughtered. Venatattiha ˁal kol-Yehuda beyad melech Bavel — I will hand all Judah over to Babylon.
5: Venatatti et kol-ochon haˁir hazze veˁet kol yegiˁah veˁet kol yqaroteha — I will give over all the wealth of this city, all its gains, all its valuables (yĕqārôt, precious things). The threefold enumeration — goods (ḥōsen), labor-products (yĕgîˁâ), precious things (yĕqārôt) — comprehensively describes the accumulated wealth of Jerusalem that will be stripped away. Leoyhvehem — to their enemies: the transfer of accumulated covenant-era prosperity to Babylon is itself a judgment on the misuse of wealth.
6: Veˁatta Pashhur vekhol yoshhvey vetekha telku bashvi — and you, Pashhur, with all who live in your household, will go into exile (šeḇî). The oracle closes with the personalized fate of Pashhur: Bavel tavo vesham tamut vesham tiqqaver — you will go to Babylon and there you will die and there you will be buried. Vekhol-ohavecha asher nibbeta lahem basheqer — and all your friends to whom you gave false prophecies (šeqer, falsehood). The priestly official who thought the institutional prophetic channels were sufficient (cf. 18:18) is himself identified as one of those false voices.
7: Pittitani YHWH vaʾepat hazaqtani vattukal — you deceived me, YHWH, and I was deceived; you overpowered me and prevailed. Pittâ (Piel, to persuade/entice/deceive) is the same root used in Exod 22:16 (enticing a virgin) and 1 Kings 22:21 (the spirit volunteering to entice Ahab's prophets) — Jeremiah accuses YHWH of the kind of persuasion that leads one into an unwanted situation. Ḥāzaq (to overpower, seize) adds the element of coercion: not just deceptive persuasion but irresistible force. This is the most theologically daring verse in all of Jeremiah's confessions — and in all of OT prayer. Hayiti litshoq kol-hayom kol-yitshaqi — I have become a laughingstock all day; everyone mocks me. The contrast with the call promise (v8: I am with you to rescue you) is at its most acute here.
8: Ki midei adabber etzˁaq hamas veshhod eshmaˁ — every time I speak, I must cry out Violence! Destruction! (ḥāmās wāšōd). The two nouns are the standard Jeremianic vocabulary for covenant violation and its consequence: ḥāmās is the violence that breaks social bonds, šōd is the resulting devastation. Ki haya devar YHWH li lhherpa uliqlas kol-hayom — the word of YHWH has become for me a reproach and mockery all day. What should be the prophet's glory — to bear the divine word — has become his social destruction.
9: Veamrti lo-ˁazkrennu velo-adabber ˁod bishmo — I thought, I will not mention him, I will speak no more in his name. The decision to abandon the prophetic vocation is consciously formed. Vehaya velibbi keˁesh boˁeret ˁatsur beˁatsmotai — then it was in my heart like a burning fire shut up in my bones (bōʿeret, blazing, from bāˁar). The fire-metaphor for prophetic compulsion is unique to this passage; the word of YHWH is not merely a message but a physical force that burns within the prophet. Nilˁeyti kalkol velo-uchal — I was weary trying to hold it in (kālaʾ, to restrain, hold back) and I could not. The irresistibility of the prophetic calling is dramatized through the failure of self-suppression.
10: Ki shamˁeti dibbat rabbim magor missaviv — for I hear the whispering of many: Magor Missavib! The name Jeremiah gave Pashhur (v3) now returns as the crowd's taunt against Jeremiah himself — the ultimate irony: the prophet of Terror-on-Every-Side is now surrounded by terror on every side. Kol enosh shhlomi shomrey tsalˁi — all my friends are watching for me to stumble (šōmĕrê ṣalˁî, watchers for my stumbling/limping). The intimate circle of trusted companions has become a surveillance network.
11: VeYHWH ˁitti kegibor ˁarits — but YHWH is with me like a powerful warrior (gibbôr ʿārîṣ, mighty/dreadful warrior). The contrast with vv7-10 is stark — from YHWH as deceiver to YHWH as warrior. ˁal-ken rodˁefai yikkashlu velo yuchlu — therefore my persecutors will stumble and not prevail: the stumbling word (kāšal) is the same used of the watchmen who stumble (v10 — tsalˁî). What the enemies wait for Jeremiah to do, they will do themselves. Boshu meod ki lo hiskilu — they will be utterly shamed because they have not succeeded.
12: YHWH tsvaot bochhen tsaddiq roeh klayyot valev — O YHWH of hosts, who tests the righteous, who sees the kidneys and heart. Klāyôt (kidneys) and lēḇ (heart) are the two internal organs associated respectively with emotional life (kidneys = the seat of deep feeling, longing, and conscience) and rational/volitional life (heart = mind and will) in Hebrew anthropology. Testing these two organs means examining the whole inner person. This is the same self-description YHWH gives in 17:10 — Jeremiah appeals to the very divine attribute that makes his own case credible.
13: Shiru laYHWH halelu et YHWH ki hitsil et nefesh evyon miyad mereˁim — sing to YHWH, praise YHWH! For he has rescued the life of the poor (ʾeḇyôn) from the hand of evildoers. The sudden shift to praise after the complaint and imprecation follows the psalm-lament genre pattern (cf. Ps 22: extreme complaint followed by praise). The poor (ʾeḇyôn) who is rescued is implicitly Jeremiah himself — the word indicates social vulnerability and dependence on YHWH alone.
14: Arur hayom asher yulladti vo — cursed be the day I was born. The self-curse following the praise (v13) is disorienting — the lament resurfaces even after the praise. The parallel with Job 3:3 is close: Job also curses the day of his birth in terms of cosmic reversal (let that day be darkness). ʾārûr is the standard curse formula of Deuteronomy 27-28 — Jeremiah applies the covenant curse-language to the moment of his own existence.
15: Arur haish asher bissar et-avi lemor yullad lecha ben zachar sammach sammeaḥahu — cursed be the man who brought the news to my father saying, A son has been born to you, making him very glad. The curse extends to the birth announcement — one of the most joyful events in ancient Near Eastern life. The birth of a son was an occasion of celebration (cf. Jer 31:13) precisely because sons perpetuated family name, land, and economic security. Jeremiah curses the very cultural institution his birth represented.
16: Vehaya haish hahu kheˁarim asher hafach YHWH velo nicham — let that man be like the cities that YHWH overthrew without relenting (wĕlōʾ niḥām). The cities overthrown without YHWH relenting are Sodom and Gomorrah (cf. the same verb hāpaḵ in Gen 19:25 — YHWH overturned/overthrew those cities). The destruction is total and irreversible — Jeremiah wishes the same fate on the man who announced his birth.
17: Asher lo memotani merachem — because he did not kill me (mēmîtanî, cause-me-to-die) in the womb. The wish for prenatal death is the extreme form of the birth-curse: better not to exist than to exist in this prophetic suffering. Vattehi imi qibri verachamah hora ˁolam — so that my mother would have been my grave and her womb forever large with me: the womb as grave is an image of existence arrested before its beginning — non-existence as the wished-for alternative to prophetic calling.
18: Lamma zeh yatsati merachem lirˁot ˁamal veyagon veyiklu veboshot yomay — why did I come out of the womb to see trouble and sorrow (ˁāmāl wĕyāgôn), with my days ending in disgrace (bōšet)? The confession ends without resolution — unlike the psalm-lament that typically ends in trust or praise, the final word of Jeremiah's darkest confession is bōšet (shame/disgrace). The literary abruptness is intentional: the prophet's suffering is not neatly resolved, and the text does not force closure on genuine anguish.
